retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: First described in 1875 by Wilhelm Heinrich Erb and Carl Friedrich Otto Westphal, the deep tendon reflex (DTR) is essential in examining and diagnosing neurologic disease. [1] Deep tendon reflexes or, more accurately, the 'muscle stretch reflex' can aid in evaluating neurologic disease affecting afferent nerves, spinal cord synaptic connections, motor nerves, and descending motor pathways. Proper technique and interpretation of results are crucial in achieving a proper distinction between upper and lower motor neuron pathologic processes such as multiple sclerosis (MS), amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), spinal cord injuries, and spinal muscular atrophies, with the presence of hyporeflexia or hyperreflexia considered a 'hard sign' of neurologic dysfunction. There are five primary deep tendon reflexes: biceps, brachioradialis, triceps, patellar, and ankle.

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The physical examination may reveal normal muscle power because of the fluctuating disease pattern. In such cases, repeated or sustained muscle contractions can demonstrate weakness. Improvement is seen after a period of rest or application of ice (ice-pack test) to the muscle group involved. The pupils, deep tendon reflexes, and sensory examinations are normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: What is 'normal' typically depends on the patient's history and past documented reflex grade. Abnormality is suggested when asymmetric reflexes are found. Once the examiner obtains a reflex on one side, they should test the same reflex on the opposite side for comparison.

retrieved_docs.title: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: LEMS is a B-cell–mediated autoimmune neurological disorder specifically targeting the cell surface antigen (VGCC). The VGCC-IgG antibody is central to this disorder and directly contributes to its pathogenesis, leading to compromised neuromuscular transmission and manifesting as observable muscle weakness. Profoundly diminished deep tendon reflexes serve as a distinctive hallmark.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: To provide a standard scale for evaluating deep tendon reflexes, in 1993, the National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS) proposed a grading scale from 0 to 4. [2] This scale has been validated and is universally accepted. [3] NINDS grading of deep tendon reflexes. [4] 0: Reflex absent 1: Reflex small, less than normal, includes a trace response or a response brought out only with reinforcement 2: Reflex in the lower half of a normal range 3: Reflex in the upper half of a normal range 4: Reflex enhanced, more than normal, includes clonus if present, which optionally can be noted in an added verbal description of the reflex In some instances, a plus sign (+) is written after the number. When discussing DTRs, adding or omitting a plus sign does not change the meaning of the reflex grade observed.

retrieved_docs.title: Brachioradialis Reflex -- Issues of Concern -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The evocation of deep tendon reflexes forms a crucial part of the neurological examination as it reveals information about the status of the portions of the nervous system that contribute to the reflexes tested. [12] Additionally, reflex testing is a part of the clinical examination for myelopathy. Specifically, this examination includes the Hoffman test, clonus, deep tendon reflex testing, Babinski sign, inverted supinator sign, hand withdrawal reflex testing, and suprapatellar quadriceps reflex testing. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The absolute grading intervals are subjective, based on the clinician’s experience of all previous normal reflexes, but are particularly useful when discrepancies are found between different joints in the same patient. Symmetrical reflexes within the 1+ to 3+ range are generally considered normal as long as upper or lower motor neuron signs do not accompany them, or there is increasing briskness with a descent down the spinal cord, which could indicate spinal cord damage between the diminished and hyperreflexic levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Patients With Myasthenia Gravis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with myasthenia gravis typically experience worsening muscle weakness as the day progresses or after physical exercise or exertion, with symptoms improving after rest. Deep tendon reflexes remain intact, and autonomic dysfunction is uncommon. However, as the disease advances, respiratory muscle involvement becomes a significant concern, increasing the risk of perioperative respiratory compromise.

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Several types of reflexes can be tested as part of a physical examination and these all reveal something about the status of the elements of the nervous system that contribute to their functioning. They have been used for over a century as part of a routine neurological examination due to their safety, low cost, predictive value, and ability to be performed rapidly, even without specialized equipment. This article will focus on the “deep tendon reflexes” which are more appropriately named — and will be referred to herein — as muscle stretch reflexes (MSR).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Testing the DTR is quick and easy but requires a proper technique for a reliable response. The examiner taps the muscle's tendon with an examination hammer, and the response is observed and graded. For effective and reliable results, the patient should be as relaxed as possible. If the patient thinks about the test or has a rigid posture, its integrity is limited.

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Technique or Treatment -- NINDS Scale:
retrieved_docs.content: 0: Reflex absent 1: Reflex small, less than normal; includes a trace response or a response brought out only with reinforcement 2: Reflex in the lower half of the normal range 3: Reflex in the upper half of the normal range 4: Reflex enhanced, more than normal; includes clonus if present, which optionally can be noted in an added verbal description of the reflex [7] Plus or minus can indicate that the reflex was in between whole grades. Occasionally “5” is used to denote sustained clonus. One-half can alternatively indicate reinforcement was used.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Introduction -- Brachioradialis Reflex
retrieved_docs.content: Muscle involved: brachioradialis Nerve supply: radial Segmental innervation: C5-C6

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The DTR is used to assess the integrity of the motor system and provides information on the condition of upper and lower motor neurons. A hypoactive or absent reflex will be noted if a patient has an injury or a disease involving a lower motor neuron (nerve roots or peripheral nerves). A hyperactive reflex will be present if the lesion or injury involves the upper motor neuron (brain, brainstem, or spinal cord). In severe chronic cases, usually associated with spasticity, clonus can be seen, which is the involuntary and rhythmic contraction of muscles caused by a lesion in the descending motor neurons. [14] Clonus is commonly seen in patients with stroke, spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy, or multiple sclerosis and can also occur after ingesting large amounts of serotonergic agents. [15] While hyperreflexia can be a normal finding, especially if bilateral, clonus, if present, is never a normal finding and requires further workup.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Introduction -- Triceps Reflex
retrieved_docs.content: Muscle involved: triceps brachii Nerve supply: radial Segmental innervation: C7-C8

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 181
retrieved_docs.content: diseases, genetic neuropathies, and distinct syndromes. This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2499
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other disorders of nervous system” encompasses a diverse group of peripheral nerve and neuromuscular conditions. It is used to classify the nonspecific term “تكزز,” drug‑induced polyneuropathy (Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik), idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب), multifocal neuropathy associated with malignant disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), multifocal neuropathy linked to collagen‑vascular disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني), chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي), and myasthenic syndrome (وهن عضلي وبيل). By grouping these varied entities under “Other disorders of nervous system,” clinicians are reminded that many neuropathic and neuromuscular disorders fall outside the more common diagnostic categories, prompting comprehensive evaluation and tailored management for patients presenting with unexplained nerve or muscle dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 269
retrieved_docs.content: concepts (pain, paralysis) with anatomical sites (peripheral nerves, specific cranial and spinal nerves, tendon sheaths, synovium), disease entities (headache, ocular disorders, the extensive list of neuropathies, muscular disorders, entrapment syndromes, rare hereditary and inflammatory conditions), and procedural interventions (nerve incision, decompression), providing clinicians with a comprehensive taxonomy for precise diagnosis, coding, and patient‑centered management of nervous‑system and sense‑organ disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1058
retrieved_docs.content: peripheral nerve involvement is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, monitoring and comprehensive patient care across the diverse disorders classified under Other disorders of the nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: Hemiplegia and hemiparesis are clinical conditions that fall under the broader category of paralysis; they involve loss of motor function on one side of the body, with hemiplegia denoting complete weakness and hemiparesis indicating partial weakness. Recognizing these disorders as subtypes of paralysis helps clinicians diagnose the extent of neurological impairment and tailor rehabilitation and treatment strategies accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other disorders of nervous system” serves as an umbrella classification for a diverse group of neuropathic conditions. It includes polyneuropathy that results from medication use, multiple‑nerve‑bundle neuropathies that occur in malignant diseases, and similar multifocal neuropathies associated with collagen‑vascular disorders. Severe or high‑risk neuropathies are also placed within this group, as are chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathies that involve both nerves and roots and lead to demyelination. Mononeuritis, a single‑nerve inflammation, and lower‑limb reflex sympathetic dystrophy (a painful, reflex‑driven condition of the extremities) are likewise classified under “Other disorders of nervous system.” Additionally, the term “تكزز” is listed as a subclass within this broader category, reflecting the inclusion of various language‑specific descriptors for these neurological disorders. Together, these classifications highlight the wide spectrum of peripheral and central nervous system pathologies that are grouped under “Other disorders of nervous system,” aiding clinicians in diagnosis, coding, and management of these heterogeneous conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2193
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a variety of distinct neuropathic and neuromuscular conditions. Within this category, اعتلال الأعصاب السكري refers to diabetic neuropathy, a common complication of chronic hyperglycemia that can cause sensory loss, pain, and autonomic dysfunction. اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب designates an idiopathic progressive neuropathy of unknown cause, characterized by gradual worsening of peripheral nerve function. اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني describes multiple neuropathies that occur secondary to collagen vascular diseases, linking systemic autoimmune inflammation with peripheral nerve damage. اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة denotes a severe form of neuropathy in which extensive nerve injury leads to profound motor and sensory deficits. اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي مزيل للميالين identifies a demyelinating inflammatory polyneuropathy, in which immune‑mediated attack on myelin sheaths produces widespread weakness and paresthesia. الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي refers to reflex sympathetic dystrophy of the lower limb (complex regional pain syndrome type I), a painful autonomic disorder that follows trauma or surgery. Finally, وهن عضلي وبيل captures a muscular weakness/atrophy syndrome, reflecting primary muscle involvement that may coexist with or mimic peripheral nerve disease. Recognizing that all of these entities are classified under Other disorders of nervous system aids clinicians in organizing differential diagnoses, selecting appropriate investigations, and tailoring multidisciplinary management strategies for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Dantrolen and baklofen are classified as centrally acting muscle relaxants, a group of agents that reduce skeletal muscle tone by acting on the central nervous system. These medications are used to manage conditions characterized by excessive muscle contraction, such as spasticity and certain movement disorders, and their shared mechanism of central action underlies their therapeutic role in alleviating muscle-related symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2758
retrieved_docs.content: Baclofen, a GABA‑B receptor agonist commonly employed as a muscle relaxant, may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis. In patients with multiple sclerosis, baclofen can help alleviate spasticity and muscle stiffness, which are frequent neurological manifestations of the disease. By reducing excessive neuronal firing in the spinal cord, baclofen contributes to improved motor function and comfort, making it a valuable therapeutic option for managing the motor symptoms associated with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2582
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of the nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that includes a variety of specific neuropathic conditions. Within this category are “تكزز,” diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري), idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب), multiple neuropathy associated with malignant disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), severe neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة), chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy with root involvement (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), mononeuritis multiplex (التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد), and lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي). By classifying these diverse entities under the umbrella of “Other disorders of nervous system,” clinicians can recognize that they share a common neuro‑anatomical focus while differing in etiology, severity, and clinical presentation, which aids in systematic evaluation, differential diagnosis, and tailored patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2241
retrieved_docs.content: Incision of nerve is classified as a type of peripheral nerve decompression, indicating that it falls under the broader category of decompression procedures targeting peripheral nerves.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that is classified as تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب السكري, اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني, اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة, اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين, التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد, الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي, and وهن عضلي وبيل. These classifications encompass a range of neuropathic conditions, including diabetic neuropathy, multifocal neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease, severe neuropathy, chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, mononeuritis multiplex, reflexive sclerosis of the lower limb, and muscular and peripheral weakness. Recognizing that these diverse entities fall under the umbrella of other disorders of the nervous system aids clinicians in organizing differential diagnoses, guiding appropriate investigations, and tailoring management strategies for patients presenting with complex neurological symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2390
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a diagnostic category that comprises a diverse group of neuropathic and inflammatory conditions. Within this category are the entities تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب (idiopathic progressive neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد الكحولي (multifocal alcoholic neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة (severe neuropathy), اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين (chronic multifocal inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد (multifocal mononeuritis), الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflexive atrophic changes of the lower limb), and وهن عضلي وبيل (muscular and peripheral weakness). Together, these conditions illustrate the breadth of disorders classified under other nervous system diseases, highlighting variations in etiology, severity, and anatomical distribution that are important for accurate diagnosis and targeted patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 273
retrieved_docs.content: pathway. Additional neurosurgical considerations include إحصار عصبي عضلي (neuromuscular blockade) and the spectrum of related neuropathologies such as diffuse encephalitis around the axis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور). Overall, the interconnected knowledge emphasizes that استسقاء الدماغ is a central nervous system disorder with defined anatomical locations, characteristic clinical associations (especially with dementia), specific neuroimaging modalities (CT head and abnormal brain echo), and a set of neurosurgical interventions essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and comprehensive patient care.
